Taken together, prior findings do not conclusively establish the effectiveness of spatial audio in UAV teleoperation, but instead highlight unresolved questions regarding (i) perceptual learn- ing, (ii) ecological validity of sensing integration, and (iii) principled sonification design.

10 Limitations While the present study provides evidence that spatialised auditory feedback can improve ob- stacle avoidance performance in UAV teleoperation, several limitations should be considered when interpreting the results.
Real-world UAV operations often involve moving obstacles and hazards that may emerge unpredictably outside the operator's field of view.

In many skill acquisition tasks, performance improvements follow a learning curve in which rapid early gains are followed by diminishing returns as participants approach a stable level of proficiency.

9.3 Audio Feedback Enhances Performance Without Workload Reduction Although the preceding analyses demonstrate consistent improvements in objective performance metrics under the auditory feedback condition, it remains unclear whether these behavioural gains correspond to changes in perceived task difficulty.

Taken together, these results indicate that spatialised auditory cues produced measurable improvements in task performance and safety without fundamentally changing the underlying control strategy employed by participants.

As participants accumulated experience, they were likely able to anticipate obstacle encounters more effectively, initiate avoidance manoeuvres earlier, and execute control inputs with greater precision.
These results therefore suggest that future teleoperation studies should adopt more explicit strategies for modelling and controlling learning dynamics.
11 Conclusion This study investigated whether spatialised auditory feedback can improve obstacle awareness during indoor UAV teleoperation under limited visual conditions.

Significant log-trial coefficients were present across several objective performance metrics as well as multiple NASA- TLX workload components, indicating that participants became both more efficient and less cognitively burdened as they gained experience with the task.
Obstacles may move, appear unpredictably, or emerge from regions outside the operator's immediate field of view.

Further studies involving longer training durations or repeated sessions across multiple days would therefore be valuable for examining how the effectiveness of auditory obstacle aware- ness cues evolves as operators transition from early learning stages toward more experienced teleoperation behaviour.
Although suboptimal nav- igation strategies may introduce detours, there exists a structural lower bound on achievable distance determined by the layout itself.
These observations suggest that the efficacy of spatial audio in UAV teleoperation was not definitively established, but rather left open important methodological questions.

To address this problem, a system-level experimental framework was developed consisting of a psychoacoustically grounded audio-based obstacle alert system, a simulation environment incorporating realistic obstacle de- tection latency, and a controlled experimental protocol aligned with the stated research ques- tions.
Active time exhibited the largest learning coefficient (-18.71, p < 0.001), indicating that participants completed the navigation task substantially faster as trials progressed.

Moreover, collision events often introduce discrete interruptions, including abrupt stops and corrective manoeuvres, which can disproportionately inflate overall duration.

By providing continuous proximity information through sound, the proposed feedback mechanism enables operators to detect obstacles earlier and recover more effectively from navigation errors, highlighting the po- tential of multisensory feedback systems to support safer and more efficient UAV teleoperation.

9.1.1 Performance Improvements Over Trials Strong learning effects were observed across repeated trials, with the mixed-effects models in Table 1 indicating statistically significant log-trial coefficients for active time, distance travelled, and collision duration.
These improvements occurred independently of any interface modification, demonstrating that repeated exposure alone can substantially influence both behavioural and subjective outcomes.
Fourteen participants performed navigation tasks under visual-only and visual-plus-audio conditions.
Rather than dismissing auditory feedback, this approach seeks to more rigorously characterise the con- ditions under which spatial sonification can effectively support indoor UAV teleoperation.

By using a common input device with discrete commands, the experiment aimed to isolate the perceptual effects of the obstacle awareness feedback system rather than differences in operator proficiency with particular control interfaces.

These learning trends highlight the importance of considering practice effects when interpreting ex- perimental results, as improvements may arise from skill acquisition over time rather than from the experimental intervention alone.

Future work could therefore evaluate the system in more complex flight scenarios involving moving obstacles, three- dimensional navigation, and dynamically evolving environments in order to better characterise its effectiveness in realistic teleoperation contexts.

Properly accounting for these dynamics is essential to ensure that observed performance differences can be confidently attributed to the interface intervention rather than to the natural progression of operator skill.

While randomisation can reduce systematic bias in the presentation of experimental conditions, the magnitude of the learning effects observed in the present study suggests that this approach alone may be insufficient.
